speaker: interviewer
text: --'95. We're in Sydney, Australia. Rolling and cameras ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Frank, you were telling me about what happened to your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. With the story of my brother, let me first finish what happened with my father and my mother. They survived by-- by buying false documents, by buying Christian documents. And my father always-- whenever he was stopped by-- by Hungarian Nazi party members on the street, who are you, he showed his Christian document. But still-- he looked pretty Jewish. See, that was his problem. Well, one day, they just were ready to shoot him. I think you still look a Jew who has false documents. But he always carried with him a-- a very faded photograph of the Hungarian Nazi party leader, a certain Mr. [? Salashe ?], who was like Hitler in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [? Salashe ?] is standing there with his arrow cross, you know, swastika, and surrounded by his top 20 aids   and ministers. And he carried this photo on him-- just never know, it might [INAUDIBLE]. And when this man stopped him in the middle of the street, he says, I'll show [INAUDIBLE], you think I'm a Jew, huh? He says, yeah, you look Jewish to me. Well, look at this. You can see His Majesty, the fuhrer-- Hungarian fuhrer. Look at this photograph. You see him there? Yeah, sure. Well, look at the third man there. That's me. So behave yourself. Oh, I'm sorry. Yes. He-- he was saluting him. So, I'm terribly sorry. It will never happen again. Well, OK. You watch out next time. You don't arrest people who are good Christians like me. He said, OK. That was-- that was one of my father's favorite happenings.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, we-- now the big task was-- my father met me and we lived on the other side of the Danube. So we had to cross the Danube on foot. There are no bridges, of course. We had to go through the long way, pontoon bridge across the Danube and up the hill where-- where we lived. And now he was very worried. My father was very worried that the sudden-- sudden good news might-- might kill my mother. Very happen news can also cause a heart attack. So we come up here, about 100 meters from our house where we lived, up-- in an uphill street. He said, look, I go forward. And I just try to gently prepare her a bit. And you don't come. Give me about 15 minutes, 15 to 20 minutes. And then you can come up slowly. So he went up, and he told my mother, [LAUGHS] who was in the kitchen. He said, look, I got some-- I got some fairly good news from you. I-- I met somebody who said that our son Frank is much better, really. He's improving.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, don't tell me, are you sure? Yes, he's better. He's-- he can move, and he can-- oh, and when do you think we can see him? Well, we don't know. But I wouldn't be surprised if he would arrive home even next week maybe. You never know. And then he   just went and just washed my hand. He went out and looked out, am I coming already? And I started to come. He said, he can come slowly.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, look, you better sit down, because you might have some very happy news. He might come next week, or he might even come this week. As a matter of fact, he's coming right now. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So, I came in. And oh, you can imagine. They put me to bed. They called about five doctors. For the first three months, they gave me every day one kilogram of sugar to fill up my blood sugar. I don't know. That's what they gave me. And then they took me to all sorts of doctors. I had to get dentures, of course. But first they had to cure all the pus and everything. So I couldn't open my mouth, in fact, because it was all-- the pus was all grown together, and they had to make operations to be able to open the mouth. They could only open it that much. So then dentures, then operations, and so forth. And then, because my mother was a fantastic cook, she brought me back to life. And six months later, I still couldn't get-- I was still in my room, lying in my bed. I could walk a little bit and go back to bed and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my pre-war girlfriend came out to visit me. And my mother went out from the room, left us alone. And I could just lie there, and she was sitting there. And I couldn't-- I couldn't do a thing, couldn't even kiss her. She went out just in case we want to get closer. And anyhow, it took about a full year before I started to go-- I was never as well as before the war, never ever. Before the war, I was a skiing champion, and I was swimming and, you know, everything. I had to live very carefully through my life. Ever since then, I had to take all the precautions. I can't take part in any-- I could never play tennis or all the other sports which other people play. But with careful living, I survived up till now. Now, what my brother was doing, was in November, he was in a labor camp around Budapest. And in November, when the worst times, when Jews were shot on the street and everywhere, the really-- the-- the hooligans, 16, 18-year-old boys with machine guns killed everybody, and then the order was for every Jew between 16 and 66 to march out of Hungary to-- to Mauthausen. They didn't know what-- they ended up in Mauthausen who wasn't shot on the way. And the reports came back that the people are-- got shot on the way and tens of thousands were shot on the way. And then my brother said, I'm not going to go there. I'm going to hide here. And he was hiding in different places. And eventually, there-- there was a group, a-- a fascist group called the [? Pronay ?] group, which specialized in murdering Jews, visiting hospitals, because many of these Jews went into private hospitals pretending that they are sick or after operation and pretending to escape the march in the snow in November.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this group went to the hospitals, pulled them out from the bed, shot them on the spot, or marched them down to the Danube and machine gunned them. They went to Jewish houses. There were Jewish houses with Magen David. They marched them out in their night-- nightgowns, women and children, to the Danube and shot them into the icy cold water.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this group was very strong. And-- and my brother and 10 of his friends, he organized-- he printed false documents that they are Christians. And they joined up this murderous group, 11 Jewish boys, all 19, 20 year old. And they got some machine guns, and they went to the hospitals. And there they talked with the old Jewish barristers and doctor-- elderly people, who were-- you know, company directors, you know, the cream of society. And they told them, look, take us from here. Pretend that you are marching us to the Danube. And on the way, halfway down, let us go to some different Jewish houses, and then we disappeared. You can go back and tell them that you shot them, and you want the next group. And they did it. For 10 or 11 days, they went around, went into this Jewish house, and said, all Jews out from the bed. And then they said, don't worry. It's OK. [LAUGHS] They just whispered it to the others. And 11 of them with machine guns, with the swastika. And it went well, and they saved hundreds and hundreds of people. But then one day, they were marching like this, or they're having lunch somewhere on the streets, or in a-- espresso or a milk barrel, whatever. And then one of the school boys went past who knew my brother, knew that he is a Jewish boy. And there he was in his swastika uniform. And a couple of them went past and said, hey. And then he went away. He came back with 20 other policemen. He said, these other people, I know a couple of them. They are for sure Jew boys. And they arrested them. And they couldn't fight. I don't know how the arrest was. I don't know. We know the newspaper reports that they were in the-- in the jail. There was a-- a court proceeding, high treason, Jews dressed up as, blah, blah, blah. That's high treason against the country. They should have been out digging trenches or doing slave labor. Instead of that, they were busy saving the enemies of our people, saving Jews. You know. So that's a capital punishment. And they asked for the death penalty. They were beaten to pulp. They were terribly tortured. And after eight days, the newspapers reported that 11 Jewish traitors, executed by hanging. That was on the 5th of December. The Russians entered Budapest on the 24th of December. So by 19 days, the-- these 11 Jewish boys got executed. And after the war, they made them officers of the army and made this special funeral. And so that was-- this was my brother. So-- that was hard. So-- so to end the story of Hungary, what happened is the Communists gradually took over everything. And when they realized-- we realized that life wouldn't be very good in Hungary because the Communists are nationalizing. [DRINKS] Anyhow, we tried to escape, eventually, from Hungary. And by the time, the escape was very difficult because they brought their Iron Curtain. It was the electrified wires and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all the difficulties we had.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, I tried to escape. First time I got caught, I was beaten up, thrown into the Communist jail, Russian jail. It looked like I was being sent to Siberia or some other place. I got five years' jail. And then it was terrible in the Russian prison. Starvation again, for six months.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then my father, who still had fairly good connections, he bribed the commander of the prison and gave him a huge amount of money, like you would say $100,000 or something like that, in gold, and let my son out. We'll cover-- we'll make it two weeks' medical treatment for defective kidneys, which I  really had-- bad, you know, very bad. And so he let me out, and they put me free. And the first thing I do, they organize in the meantime, other man who specialize in smuggle-- smuggling people across the border-- not to the Hungarian Austrian border, but first to Czechoslovakia. Czechoslovakia was, at that stage, moderate Communism than Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: So first we had to cross the river, go to Czechoslovakia, which I did. Went to Bratislava. And from there, through the open fields at nighttime, we had to march 15, 20 kilometers, which we did. And then eventually, by September 1949, I arrived to Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was a group of 20 Jewish boys and girls who marched like that, lying in the fields and going through potato and wheat fields, you know. And they said that if you ever get through the Russian lines, and we are still alive, and they don't shoot us on the border, and we get to Vienna, there is an American sector, of course, where we wanted to go. American, British, French, and Russian. And if you get to the American sector alive, then we'll go up to the first American soldiers we can find, and we shall-- we shall-- we shall lie down in the dust and we shall kiss his boots and lick his shoelaces. So we are sure we'll do it, because we were so terribly desperate to get out from Eastern Europe. And eventually, we did it. We arrived to Vienna. We were put in the [INAUDIBLE], and there was about a thousand people on the one floor that just didn't bother us. We had enough to eat. They gave us food and everything. And then we went out for a little walk. And at the corner of the first intersection, fair enough, there was a huge black American military policeman, kept, you know, something with MP on his arm. We walked up to him in our rags-- you know, filthy, dirty by lying in the potato fields.

speaker: interviewee
text: We go up. We lie down in front of him. What are you doing, you crazy? You know, we made a promise that if we ever reach Americans, we're going to lick your boots. He said, all right, then men, go on. Keep licking. It needs a bit of licking anyhow. And kept licking his boots. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we had to bribe Russians, and he had to get a truck. We had to bribe the captain of the Russian people to get through the border. It was the line between the Russian lines-- one half was Russia, through the middle of Austria, and the other half was already America. On one side, they were Russian soldiers drinking their vodka. On the other side were Americans, reading their stars and stripes magazines and having lollipops and chocolates. The Russians had no chocolates. They had vodka. And we bribed [? this-- ?] they're staying in the-- on the train. And then the truck, said, gee, it's very dicey. I don't know, if we get out through this line again, I'll be the happiest. I don't mind if I get to-- because I had Australian papers waiting for me in Paris. If I get to Australia, I don't mind if I'm sitting on the roadside and just polish people's boots. I'm happy to do that, just to get out from the Russians. And then we got through to the American sector. And I had to buy a falsified passport to put-- to go through Austria to Germany. In Germany, I had to buy a Danish SS man's passport who was no longer an SS man. He was just a peaceful-- peace-loving civilian. And he sold his passport for $500. We took out the photo, put in my photo, a Danish passport. And then we made a stamp and put in an official Danish government stamp. It was easy to do it. Anybody can make a rubber stamp, a rubber stamp maker, another $50. So-- and then I picked a good train at night, and we went through-- German-- midnight train took me from Munchen, Bavaria, to Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I arrived to Paris. In Paris, this friend of mine already had Australian landing permit. So all I had to do is go to the joint and get some money to-- for food. And they put me on a ship in Genoa, on a refugee ship. And the refugee ship took me to Fremantle, to Perth, and then to Melbourne, and then to the [INAUDIBLE] refugee camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then eventually I arrived to Sydney around the 20th of March, 1950, exactly 45 years ago, with $3. I still-- I received $5 on the ship for every refugee. And I still had $3. I saved up three from it so I could buy a sandwich and a lemonade. [LAUGHS] And I arrived to Sydney. So there you are. I'm in Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, I had one friend in Sydney who-- who rented me a room at Kings Cross, in a boarding house. I stayed there two nights. Then I-- I got a-- I improved. I improved a bit. Then I found another friend of mine who was living in Coogee in a little flat. And he said, you can have this room for 4 guineas a week. I got this room. Then I got a job at Davy Jones selling cigarettes and matches. Later on, I advanced to selling pipes and lighters. It was a pretty good job. They paid 8 pounds a week less tax, so I got 7 pounds and 6 shillings and threepence for clear money. And then I-- I married my former girlfriend who I met in-- it's a long  story-- in camps. I married her. We couldn't find a witness, so it was just a special JP who witnessed it. And then the problem was where to live. So we went to Ocean Street [INAUDIBLE], where we rented a room in a boarding house where the rain was coming in through the-- it was very dilapidated building. 5 pound a week rent. Two beds, one here.

speaker: interviewee
text: So each time I had to visit my new wife, at night I had to go through the rainy section of the room. The middle was-- rain was coming down. We had a different place to pick up the water. So I had to go through to the other side, to her bed on the other side. And we had one kitchen for about 10-- 10 families, where each one had 10 minutes we could boil some frankfurters and had a cup of tea. So that was an improvement. And then the next improvement was, another friend purchased  the house with a low deposit in [? Mossman ?]. Then we rented a room there. And then so gradually, bit by bit, I ended up living in Lane Cove in an old house. And then after seven years, I saved up enough money to buy this block of land, for which they asked  the outrageous price of 1,800 pounds. So I shelled out 1,800.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my neighbor said, still, he said, it's robbery in broad daylight. 1,800 pounds for a block of land. So-- other land is selling for 1,000, 1,000-- 1,100. I says, I like this. It's a bigger land. I was thinking about a swimming pool maybe later. You know. So that's [? all. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I bought the land. And then I saved up enough money to pay a deposit and get a loan and so forth. And then I built this house. I built this house in 1958, eight years after I arrived. I got a good, big loan. And I had to work the next 10 years to pay off the loan. [LAUGHS] So for-- gradually, I improved myself from the-- from the room in the boarding house to become-- I'm here now for 37 years in Killara.

speaker: interviewee
text: There you are. That's in a nutshell my story. I got two daughters living with me. And my wife, who divorced me, but I'll find another wife. That's another story.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any messages 50 years after the Holocaust that you'd like to give to your children and the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- I give them-- I give them many, many, many messages. But I mean, you can't-- you can't change human nature. The animal is still in-- in very many human beings. The uneducated ones, they are still full with hate. They still want to kill and murder. I don't think if you can ever improve on them. The other thing is, it's-- it's easy to be kind and polite and gentle to your fellow human beings if you live in an affluent society, where there is plenty to eat and everything is in plentiful supply with high wages. But in the countries where there is poverty and there is hunger, people are not so gentle. They will kill again and again, and they will murder minorities again and again, and I don't know what you can do about it. As long as there are hungry people and people in misery, they are not polite. They are rude. They are pushing each other. You go to Hungary-- I mean, to Russia twice. I go to Hungary every year. And people there are still just as rude, only they haven't got so many Jews to kill. But they're still-- they're still rude and still nasty to each other. And the only other thing is the other side of the story that people who go through these suffering of such hunger, they-- they-- they appreciate the everyday things much more. They don't worry about what color the curtain is. They don't get nervous breakdowns if there is a spot on the carpet or there is some dirt in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you see a cockroach, some people get nervous breakdown if there is dirt in the kitchen. I say, look, we have our-- we have bread to eat, haven't we? We have a soup? We don't have to stand out in minus 20 degrees. We-- we have clothing on. We can change our sheets. We can change our pajamas. We don't have to pick the lice. We were covered in lice and our uniform wasn't washed for six months or eight months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Working in it, sleeping in it, in Auschwitz, was always full with lice. I didn't tell you before. So if you appreciate the everyday things, that you got food to eat-- I still can't get over that you can get bananas here, any amount. I eat every day two or three, because still, every banana for me is like a miracle, because it was very hard to get bananas in Eastern Europe. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, the-- the miracle, that you-- you are left in peace and the neighbors don't announce you to the Gestapo, that they don't come in the middle of the night to arrest you for-- you know, send people away, got arrested, and killed by the millions, you know. So people leave you alone. They leave you in peace. They don't do much for you, but at least they don't-- they don't kill you. [LAUGHS] Thank you so much, Frank. Thank you so much for sharing your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: My pleasure. My pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Frank, would you like to introduce your two daughters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, certainly. On my right is Monica, just over 23 years old, a very good girl. She's a very good skier. And she went to [INAUDIBLE] University. And right now she has a job in a Lane Cove computer parts-- computer supply company. That's Monica here. And on my left, we have young Helen, just over 21 years of age. Also an excellent skier. And she finished two years of [INAUDIBLE] University. She's still studying at this stage. She's not sure yet what she wants to be, except to be a happy wife and a mother of maybe two or three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Frank, you were saying that you took your daughters back to Auschwitz or Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I took-- I took Monica once to Europe. And Helen was already twice to Europe. I took her to Mauthausen concentration camp. And I took-- I took Helen to Theresienstadt and Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Dachau.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Dachau. So she got a good-- she had a good look around there. She knows all these places pretty well. I didn't take them to Auschwitz yet because I went to Auschwitz when there was still law and order under the Communism system. Now it's a bit more dangerous in Poland. But I went back twice to Auschwitz myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel going back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, actually I-- I don't think I went there, Dad. I saw Dad's birthplace in Budapest, actually, walked through Hungary there, and that quite affected me, seeing where he grew up, he's-- where he spent his youth and his school and that kind of thing. Quite moving.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It's quite an emotional experience when you go through one of the concentration camps. And I think there was one in Theresienstadt. There were lots of-- the cemetery, you know, with each-- each tombstone they had a dedication, and it was quite moving. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you went to Mauthausen. Do you remember Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Mauthausen as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: I showed you the place where my barrack was, where I was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It just made my dad's experience seem more real, seeing where he-- where he actually was. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you feel that your father's experiences have helped you in any way with your lives, or shaped them in some way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helen, do you want to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think they made us more modest. I don't think we-- it's made us realize that we don't need so much in life. We-- you know, we-- we just need some basic things, like food and shelter. That, you know, that'll make us quite content, because that's how Dad sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose you understand that the accumulation of all sort of consumer goods doesn't necessarily bring happiness.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: As long as you got your basic needs covered.

speaker: interviewee
text: It makes you realize what's really important in life, like family, close family, and-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think our priorities in life might be different from other people our age. I'm just thinking of my friends who want to buy this and buy that, and we think, what's the point of those products? It doesn't really mean much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, people particularly, they are obsessed with accumulation of [? all ?] unnecessary consumer goods, which are not really important for life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you so much. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You can read this back to 1686.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell me-- explain your family tree there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, certainly. That's-- that's my family tree on my mother's side only. And this shows that in 1686, there was a rabbi who had several daughters and a son. And then the family lived mostly in Vienna and in Budapest, partly in Vienna, partly in Budapest. This family tree was compiled in the early 1930s. The man who compiled it died, of course, a long time ago. And my mother gave it to me about 25 years ago, before she died. She says, keep this for your grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Frank, could you please tell me about the document?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a document which I obtained from the central office of the former Auschwitz concentration camp, which is now a Polish Auschwitz state museum, which in 1980, when I first I went back after the war, they-- I walked in, I gave them  my name, and right away they could find on their computer that I was there, when I arrived, my-- my tattooed number, when I was moved from Auschwitz to Mauthausen, and my number in Mauthausen. And it also gives the date when I was liberated by the American army, in the 5th of May, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Frank, could you please explain the document?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, certainly. That's the provisional identity card, supplied by the American commander of Mauthausen, six weeks after liberation on the 28th of June, 1945, saying that I-- I'm a Hungarian citizen who was deported to first Auschwitz, then to Mauthausen, with my fingerprint on it, and giving my number. So that was the only document I had on me when I arrived back to Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: That-- this is the end of roll seven of the interview with Frank Gipps. It's the 20th of March, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia. And that's the end of the whole interview.
